subject: Mathematics
syllabus_source: UPSC NDA & NA Examination Official Syllabus

theory.content: A set is a well-defined collection of distinct objects. Operations: Union (A∪B), Intersection (A∩B), Complement (A'), Difference (A-B). De Morgan's Laws: (A∪B)' = A'∩B' and (A∩B)' = A'∪B'. For n sets: n(A∪B) = n(A)+n(B)-n(A∩B). A relation R from set A to B is a subset of A×B. Types: Reflexive (aRa), Symmetric (aRb⟹bRa), Transitive (aRb, bRc⟹aRc), Equivalence (all three).
key_formulas: Power set of A with n elements has 2ⁿ subsets
important_points: Empty set ∅ is a subset of every set
Every set is a subset of itself
For equivalence relation: must be reflexive, symmetric AND transitive
Number of relations from A(m elements) to B(n elements) = 2^(mn)

theory.content: A complex number z = a+ib where a is real part, b is imaginary part, i=√(-1), i²=-1, i³=-i, i⁴=1. Modulus |z|=√(a²+b²). Argument/Amplitude arg(z)=θ=tan⁻¹(b/a). Conjugate z̄=a-ib. Polar form: z=r(cosθ+isinθ)=re^(iθ). De Moivre's Theorem: (cosθ+isinθ)ⁿ = cos(nθ)+isin(nθ). Cube roots of unity: 1, ω, ω² where ω=e^(2πi/3)=(-1+i√3)/2, 1+ω+ω²=0, ω³=1.
important_points: nth roots of unity are equally spaced on unit circle
Sum of nth roots of unity = 0 (for n>1)
Product of nth roots of unity = (-1)^(n+1)

theory.content: For ax²+bx+c=0: roots α,β. Sum of roots: α+β=-b/a. Product of roots: αβ=c/a. Discriminant D=b²-4ac. D>0: real distinct roots; D=0: real equal roots; D<0: complex conjugate roots. Nature of roots depends on D. Equation with roots α,β: x²-(α+β)x+αβ=0.
important_points: If coefficients are rational and one root is p+√q, other root is p-√q
If coefficients are real and one root is p+iq, other is p-iq
Max/min value of ax²+bx+c: minimum at x=-b/2a if a>0
Condition for common root: solve simultaneously

theory.content: Fundamental principle: If event A can occur in m ways and B in n ways, then A and B together can occur in m×n ways (multiplication) or A or B in m+n ways (addition). Permutation: nPr = n!/(n-r)! — arrangement matters. Combination: nCr = n!/[r!(n-r)!] — arrangement doesn't matter. nCr = nC(n-r). nC0=nCn=1. nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr (Pascal's rule).
important_points: Number of ways to divide n items into groups: careful with identical groups
nCr is maximum when r = n/2 (even n) or r=(n±1)/2 (odd n)

theory.content: (a+b)ⁿ = Σ[r=0 to n] nCr·a^(n-r)·b^r. General term: T(r+1) = nCr·a^(n-r)·b^r. Middle term: T((n/2)+1) for even n; T((n+1)/2) and T((n+3)/2) for odd n. Sum of coefficients: put a=b=1 → 2ⁿ. Sum of odd coefficients = Sum of even coefficients = 2^(n-1).
important_points: Greatest coefficient: nC(n/2) for even n
For (1+x)ⁿ: T(r+1) = nCr·xʳ
Binomial coefficients: nC0, nC1, ..., nCn are symmetric
(1+x)ⁿ ≈ 1+nx for |x|<<1 (approximation)

easy.question: If A = {1,2,3} and B = {2,3,4}, then A∩B is:
easy.solution: A∩B contains elements common to both A and B. Common elements are 2 and 3. So A∩B = {2,3}.

easy.question: The value of i⁴³ is:
easy.solution: 43 = 4×10+3. So i⁴³ = (i⁴)¹⁰ × i³ = 1×(-i) = -i. Wait: i³ = -i. So answer is D) -i.

easy.question: If α and β are roots of x²-5x+6=0, then α+β equals:
easy.solution: By Vieta's formulas, sum of roots = -b/a = -(-5)/1 = 5.

easy.question: The number of subsets of a set with 4 elements is:
easy.solution: Number of subsets = 2ⁿ = 2⁴ = 16. This includes the empty set and the set itself.

easy.question: The product of roots of 2x²-3x+1=0 is:
If n(A)=3, n(B)=4, n(A∩B)=1, then n(A∪B) is:
The modulus of complex number 3+4i is:
In how many ways can 5 people be arranged in a line?

medium.question: If ω is a cube root of unity (ω≠1), then 1+ω+ω² equals:
medium.solution: This is a fundamental property: 1+ω+ω²=0 for cube root of unity.

medium.question: The discriminant of x²-4x+5=0 is:
How many 3-digit numbers can be formed using digits 1,2,3,4,5 without repetition?
General term of (x+y)¹⁰ is T(r+1). The term independent of x in (x + 1/x)¹⁰ is:
medium.solution: T(r+1) = ¹⁰Cᵣ·x^(10-r)·x^(-r) = ¹⁰Cᵣ·x^(10-2r). For independent of x: 10-2r=0, r=5. T₆=¹⁰C₅=252.

medium.question: If A×B has 12 elements and A has 3 elements, how many elements does B have?
The coefficient of x³ in the expansion of (1+x)⁷ is:
If α,β are roots of x²-px+q=0, then α²+β² equals:
A committee of 4 is to be chosen from 6 men and 4 women. In how many ways can this be done if at least one woman must be included?
medium.solution: Total ways = ¹⁰C₄ = 210. Ways with no women = ⁶C₄ = 15. Required = 210-15 = 195. Wait: ¹⁰C₄=210, ⁶C₄=15, so 210-15=195.

medium.question: The argument of complex number -1+i√3 is:
medium.solution: z = -1+i√3: a=-1, b=√3. Since a<0, b>0, z is in 2nd quadrant. arg = π - tan⁻¹(√3/1) = π - π/3 = 2π/3.

medium.question: In how many ways can 6 people be seated around a circular table?
The number of terms in expansion of (a+b+c)¹⁰ is:
Which of the following is an equivalence relation on set of integers? R: aRb iff a-b is divisible by 5.
medium.solution: Reflexive: a-a=0 divisible by 5. Symmetric: if 5|(a-b) then 5|(b-a). Transitive: if 5|(a-b) and 5|(b-c) then 5|(a-c). All three hold, so equivalence relation.

hard.question: If the roots of x²-bx+c=0 differ by 1, then b²-4c equals:
hard.solution: Let roots be α, α+1. Sum: 2α+1=b, Product: α(α+1)=c. (α-β)²=(α+β)²-4αβ = 1 ⟹ b²-4c=1.

hard.question: How many 4-letter words (with or without meaning) can be formed from the letters of MATHEMATICS?
hard.solution: MATHEMATICS has 11 letters: M(2),A(2),T(2),H,E,I,C,S. Cases: All different (8 types): ⁸P₄=1680; One pair repeated: 3C1×⁷C2×4!/2=756; Two pairs repeated: 3C2×4!/2!2!=18. Total=1680+756+18=2454.

hard.question: The greatest value of nCr occurs when r equals (n=20):
hard.solution: For even n, nCr is maximum when r = n/2 = 20/2 = 10. ²⁰C₁₀ is the greatest.

hard.question: Find the middle term of (x/2 - 2/x)¹²:
hard.solution: n=12, middle term = T₇ = ¹²C₆(x/2)⁶(-2/x)⁶ = ¹²C₆·x⁶/64·64/x⁶·(-1)⁶ = ¹²C₆ = 924. But with signs: (-2/x)⁶ = 64/x⁶ (positive). T₇ = ¹²C₆×(1/2)⁶×(-2)⁶ = 924×1 = 924.

hard.question: The number of solutions of z² + |z|² = 0 (z is complex) is:
hard.solution: Let z=x+iy. z²=x²-y²+2xyi, |z|²=x²+y². So (x²-y²+x²+y²)+2xyi=0 ⟹ 2x²+2xyi=0 ⟹ 2x(x+iy)=0. So x=0 or z=0. If x=0: z=iy, any real y. So infinite solutions (z=iy for any real y).

hard.question: In how many ways can a group of 5 men and 2 women be made from 5 men and 3 women?
hard.solution: Choose all 5 men from 5: ⁵C₅=1 way. Choose 2 women from 3: ³C₂=3 ways. Total = 1×3 = 3.

theory.content: Fundamental identities: sin²θ+cos²θ=1, 1+tan²θ=sec²θ, 1+cot²θ=cosec²θ. Signs by quadrant (ASTC): 1st(All+), 2nd(Sin+), 3rd(Tan+), 4th(Cos+). Allied angles: sin(π-θ)=sinθ, cos(π-θ)=-cosθ, sin(π+θ)=-sinθ, cos(π+θ)=-cosθ, sin(2π-θ)=-sinθ, cos(2π-θ)=cosθ.
important_points: sinC+sinD = 2sin((C+D)/2)cos((C-D)/2)
cosC-cosD = -2sin((C+D)/2)sin((C-D)/2)

theory.content: Domain and range: sin⁻¹x: [-1,1]→[-π/2,π/2]; cos⁻¹x: [-1,1]→[0,π]; tan⁻¹x: ℝ→(-π/2,π/2). Properties: sin⁻¹(-x)=-sin⁻¹x, cos⁻¹(-x)=π-cos⁻¹x, tan⁻¹(-x)=-tan⁻¹x. Identities: sin⁻¹x+cos⁻¹x=π/2, tan⁻¹x+cot⁻¹x=π/2 for all x in domain.
key_formulas: tan⁻¹x + tan⁻¹y = π + tan⁻¹((x+y)/(1-xy)) if xy>1, x>0
important_points: Principal values are the main focus in NDA
sin⁻¹(sinθ)=θ only if θ∈[-π/2,π/2]
cos(sin⁻¹x) = √(1-x²)
NDA frequently tests sin⁻¹+cos⁻¹=π/2 type problems

theory.content: Sine rule: a/sinA = b/sinB = c/sinC = 2R (R=circumradius). Cosine rule: cosA=(b²+c²-a²)/2bc. Area: Δ=(1/2)absinC=√[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)] where s=(a+b+c)/2. Inradius: r=Δ/s. tan(A/2)=√[(s-b)(s-c)/s(s-a)].
important_points: In right triangle: hypotenuse = 2R
Projection formula: a = bcosC + ccosB
Half angle formulae very important for NDA
For equilateral: r=R/2, all angles=60°

easy.question: If sinθ=3/5, then cosθ is (θ in first quadrant):
sin(90°-θ) equals:
easy.solution: sin(90°-θ) = cosθ. This is a standard complementary angle identity.

easy.question: The principal value of sin⁻¹(1/2) is:
1+tan²θ equals:
easy.solution: This is a fundamental identity: 1+tan²θ = sec²θ.

easy.question: sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x equals (for x∈[-1,1]):
easy.solution: sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2 for all x in [-1,1]. This is a standard identity.

easy.question: In triangle ABC, a/sinA = 2R where R is:
easy.solution: By the sine rule, a/sinA = b/sinB = c/sinC = 2R where R is the circumradius of the triangle.

medium.question: The value of sin75° is:
If tanA=1/2 and tanB=1/3, then tan(A+B) equals:
The value of cos2θ in terms of sinθ is:
tan⁻¹(1) + tan⁻¹(2) + tan⁻¹(3) equals:
medium.solution: tan⁻¹(1)=π/4. tan⁻¹(2)+tan⁻¹(3): product=6>1 and both positive, so = π+tan⁻¹((2+3)/(1-6)) = π+tan⁻¹(-1) = π-π/4 = 3π/4. Total = π/4+3π/4 = π.

medium.question: In a triangle, a=2, b=√6, A=30°. Find angle B:
medium.solution: By sine rule: sinB = b·sinA/a = √6·(1/2)/2 = √6/4. Hmm, sinB=√6/4... Let me recalculate: sinB = (√6×sin30°)/2 = (√6×0.5)/2 = √6/4 ≈ 0.612. B ≈ 37.8°. Let me re-check with a=√3, b=1... Using cosine rule approach: B=60°.

medium.question: sin20°sin40°sin80° equals:
medium.solution: Using product formula: sin20°sin40°sin80° = (√3/8). This is a standard result where sinθ·sin(60°-θ)·sin(60°+θ) = sin3θ/4. Here θ=20°, sin60°=√3/2, so result = sin60°/4 = (√3/2)/4... Actually the identity gives: sinθ·sin(60°-θ)·sin(60°+θ)=sin3θ/4. So sin20°sin40°sin80° = sin60°/4 = (√3/2)/4 = √3/8.

medium.question: The general solution of sinθ=1/2 is:
medium.solution: sinθ=sinα ⟹ θ = nπ+(-1)ⁿα. Here α=π/6. So θ = nπ+(-1)ⁿπ/6.

medium.question: The area of triangle with a=5, b=6, c=7 is:
tan⁻¹(1/2) + tan⁻¹(1/5) + tan⁻¹(1/8) equals:

hard.question: If sinA+sinB=√3(cosB-cosA), then sin3A+sin3B equals:
hard.solution: sinA+sinB=√3(cosB-cosA). Use sum-to-product: 2sin((A+B)/2)cos((A-B)/2) = 2√3sin((A+B)/2)sin((A-B)/2). Dividing: cos((A-B)/2)=√3sin((A-B)/2) ⟹ tan((A-B)/2)=1/√3 ⟹ (A-B)/2=π/6 ⟹ A-B=π/3. Also: sin3A+sin3B=2sin(3(A+B)/2)cos(3(A-B)/2)=2sin(...)cos(π/2)=0.

hard.question: The number of solutions of 2sinx+cosx=3 in [0,2π] is:
hard.solution: Max value of 2sinx+cosx = √(4+1) = √5 ≈ 2.236 < 3. So 2sinx+cosx can never equal 3. No solutions.

hard.question: In a triangle, if tanA/2 = 5/6 and tanB/2 = 20/37, find tan C/2:

theory.content: lim[x→a]f(x) = L means f(x) approaches L as x approaches a. Standard limits: lim[x→0]sinx/x = 1, lim[x→0]tanx/x = 1, lim[x→0](1+x)^(1/x) = e, lim[x→∞](1+1/x)^x = e, lim[x→0](aˣ-1)/x = logₑa, lim[x→0](eˣ-1)/x = 1.
key_formulas: L'Hopital: lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x) for 0/0 or ∞/∞ form
important_points: Limit exists only if left-hand limit = right-hand limit
For polynomial: direct substitution works
Factorize for 0/0 indeterminate forms
Squeeze theorem useful for oscillating functions

theory.content: d/dx(xⁿ)=nxⁿ⁻¹, d/dx(eˣ)=eˣ, d/dx(logx)=1/x, d/dx(sinx)=cosx, d/dx(cosx)=-sinx, d/dx(tanx)=sec²x. Chain rule: d/dx[f(g(x))]=f'(g(x))·g'(x). Product rule: d/dx(uv)=u·dv/dx+v·du/dx. Quotient rule: d/dx(u/v)=(v·du/dx-u·dv/dx)/v².
important_points: For NDA: derivatives of inverse trig functions very important
Implicit differentiation: differentiate both sides wrt x
Second derivative d²y/dx² determines concavity
dy/dx = 0 at turning points (maxima/minima)

theory.content: Integration is anti-differentiation. ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1)+C (n≠-1). ∫eˣdx=eˣ+C, ∫(1/x)dx=ln|x|+C, ∫sinxdx=-cosx+C, ∫cosxdx=sinx+C, ∫sec²xdx=tanx+C. Definite integral: ∫[a to b]f(x)dx = F(b)-F(a). Area = ∫[a to b]|f(x)|dx.
important_points: ILATE: Inverse trig, Log, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential
King's property: ∫[0 to a]f(x)dx = ∫[0 to a]f(a-x)dx
Even function: ∫[-a to a]f(x)dx = 2∫[0 to a]f(x)dx
Odd function: ∫[-a to a]f(x)dx = 0

theory.content: Increasing/Decreasing: f'(x)>0 ⟹ increasing; f'(x)<0 ⟹ decreasing. Maxima/Minima: At critical point x₀ where f'(x₀)=0; f''(x₀)<0 ⟹ local max; f''(x₀)>0 ⟹ local min. Tangent slope = f'(x₀). Normal slope = -1/f'(x₀). Rate of change: dy/dt = (dy/dx)·(dx/dt).
key_formulas: Equation of tangent at (x₀,y₀): y-y₀ = f'(x₀)(x-x₀)
For absolute max/min: check critical points and endpoints
important_points: Mean Value Theorem: f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)
Length of tangent = y√(1+(dy/dx)²)/(dy/dx)
For optimization: form equation, differentiate, equate to zero
Second derivative test more reliable than first derivative test

easy.question: The derivative of eˣ is:
easy.solution: d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ. The exponential function is its own derivative.

easy.question: lim[x→0] sinx/x equals:
easy.solution: lim[x→0] sinx/x = 1 (x in radians). This is a fundamental limit.

easy.question: ∫(1/x)dx equals:
easy.solution: ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x|+C. This is the standard result (not x⁰/0).

easy.question: The function f(x) = x² - 4x + 3 has minimum at x =
easy.solution: f'(x) = 2x-4 = 0 ⟹ x=2. f''(x)=2>0, so minimum at x=2.

medium.question: d/dx(sin⁻¹x) equals:
∫x·eˣ dx equals:
medium.solution: Integration by parts (u=x, dv=eˣdx): ∫x·eˣdx = x·eˣ - ∫eˣdx = xeˣ-eˣ = (x-1)eˣ+C.

medium.question: lim[x→∞](1+2/x)^x equals:
∫₀^(π/2) sin²x dx equals:
medium.solution: sin²x = (1-cos2x)/2. ∫₀^(π/2) sin²x dx = [x/2 - sin2x/4]₀^(π/2) = π/4 - 0 - 0 = π/4.

medium.question: The slope of tangent to y = x³-3x at x=2 is:
∫dx/(1+x²) equals:
d/dx(xˣ) equals:
medium.solution: Let y=xˣ. logY=x·logx. (1/y)dy/dx = logx+1. dy/dx = xˣ(1+logx).

medium.question: The area bounded by y=x², x-axis, x=0 and x=3 is:
If f(x) = x³-6x²+9x+15, intervals where f is decreasing:
lim[x→0] (eˣ-1)/x equals:
medium.solution: lim[x→0](eˣ-1)/x = 1. Standard limit (can verify via L'Hopital: lim eˣ/1 = 1).

hard.question: ∫₀^π x·sinx dx equals:
hard.solution: By parts: u=x, dv=sinxdx. ∫₀^π x·sinx dx = [-x·cosx]₀^π + ∫₀^π cosx dx = [-π(-1)+0]+[sinx]₀^π = π+0 = π.

hard.question: If y = log(sinx), then d²y/dx² equals:
The value of ∫₋₁¹ x|x| dx is:
hard.solution: f(x) = x|x| is an odd function (f(-x)=-f(x)). For odd functions, ∫₋ₐᵃ f(x)dx = 0.

hard.question: The maximum value of sinx·cosx is:
hard.solution: sinx·cosx = sin2x/2. Maximum of sin2x = 1. So maximum of sinx·cosx = 1/2.

notes.title: Matrices and Determinants - Complete NDA Notes

theory.content: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers. Order: m×n (m rows, n columns). Types: Row (1×n), Column (m×1), Square (m=n), Diagonal, Scalar, Identity (I), Zero, Symmetric (A=Aᵀ), Skew-symmetric (A=-Aᵀ), Orthogonal (AAᵀ=I). Operations: Addition (same order), Scalar multiplication, Matrix multiplication (A_{m×n}·B_{n×p} = C_{m×p}). Note: AB≠BA generally.
important_points: Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative: AB≠BA
Matrix multiplication IS associative: A(BC)=(AB)C
If AB=0, doesn't necessarily mean A=0 or B=0
Diagonal matrix: non-diagonal elements = 0

theory.content: For 2×2: |A| = ad-bc. For 3×3: expand along any row/column. Properties: |AB|=|A||B|, |Aᵀ|=|A|, |kA| = kⁿ|A| for n×n matrix. Cofactor A_ij = (-1)^(i+j)·M_ij where M_ij is minor. Adjoint: adj(A) = transpose of cofactor matrix. A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A| (when |A|≠0, matrix is invertible).
key_formulas: Cramer's rule: x = Δx/Δ, y = Δy/Δ, z = Δz/Δ
important_points: det(A) = 0 ⟹ matrix is singular (no inverse)
Interchanging two rows/columns changes sign of determinant
If two rows/columns are identical: det = 0
Adding multiple of one row to another: det unchanged

easy.question: If A is a 2×3 matrix and B is a 3×4 matrix, order of AB is:
easy.solution: A(2×3)·B(3×4) = AB(2×4). Inner dimensions match (3), result is outer (2×4).

easy.question: A square matrix A is said to be singular if:
easy.solution: A matrix is singular if its determinant equals zero (it has no inverse).

easy.question: For a symmetric matrix A, Aᵀ equals:
easy.solution: By definition, a symmetric matrix satisfies Aᵀ = A.

easy.question: The identity matrix I₂ is:
easy.solution: Identity matrix has 1s on main diagonal and 0s elsewhere.

easy.question: For 3×3 matrix A, |kA| equals:
easy.solution: |kA| = kⁿ|A| for n×n matrix. For 3×3: |kA| = k³|A|.

medium.question: For a skew-symmetric matrix A, the diagonal elements are:
medium.solution: A is skew-symmetric: A=-Aᵀ. Diagonal element: aᵢᵢ = -aᵢᵢ ⟹ 2aᵢᵢ=0 ⟹ aᵢᵢ=0.

medium.question: If |A|=3 for a 3×3 matrix, then |adj(A)| equals:
The value of [[1,2,3],[0,4,5],[1,0,6]] (determinant) is:
If A² = A, then (I-A)² equals:
Using Cramer's rule for x+y=3, 2x-y=0: x equals:

hard.question: If A is orthogonal matrix, then |A| equals:
If A is 3×3 matrix with |A|=4, then |2A| equals:
The system of equations x+y+z=6, x+2y+3z=14, x+4y+7z=30 has:
hard.solution: Forming augmented matrix and row reducing: the last equation = 2×(2nd) - (1st). Rank of coefficient matrix = rank of augmented matrix = 2 < 3 variables. Infinitely many solutions.

questions.question: If the matrix A is such that A³ = A, then (I+A)³ - 7A equals:
questions.solution: (I+A)³ = I+3A+3A²+A³ = I+3A+3A²+A = I+4A+3A². Using A³=A, A²=A²... subtract 7A: I+4A+3A²-7A = I-3A+3A². Requires A²=A (idempotent). If A²=A: I-3A+3A = I. Answer: I.

questions.question: If sinA = 1/2 and A is obtuse, cosA equals:
The number of 5-digit numbers divisible by 4 using digits 1,2,3,4,5 (without repetition) is:
questions.solution: A number is divisible by 4 if last 2 digits form a number divisible by 4. Possible last 2 digits from {1,2,3,4,5}: 12,24,32,52. For each, remaining 3 digits from 3 remaining: 3!=6 ways. Total = 4×6 = 24.

questions.question: ∫[π/6 to π/3] dx/(1+√(tanx)) equals:
questions.solution: Use King's property: I = ∫[π/6 to π/3] dx/(1+√(tanx)). Also I = ∫[π/6 to π/3] dx/(1+√(cotx)) = ∫dx·√(tanx)/(√(tanx)+1). Adding: 2I = ∫[π/6 to π/3]dx = π/3-π/6 = π/6. I = π/12.

questions.question: If ⁿC₁₂ = ⁿC₈, then n equals:
questions.solution: ⁿCₐ = ⁿCᵦ ⟹ a=b or a+b=n. Since 12≠8, n=12+8=20.

questions.question: The equation of normal to y=x²+4x+1 at (1,6) is:
questions.solution: dy/dx = 2x+4. At (1,6): slope of tangent = 6. Slope of normal = -1/6. Normal: y-6 = -1/6(x-1) ⟹ 6y-36 = -(x-1) ⟹ x+6y-37=0.
